summary.intent: Resolve review comment 0Ca, which flagged a controlled-component veto inconsistency in the FileUpload state machine — the concern was that an optimistic sync in commit_files prevented parents from vetoing file selections.
summary.outcome: After two rounds of architecture Q&A with the user, determined the component-owns-queue model was already correctly implemented; the fix was purely documentation reframing (commit_files, on_files_change, FilesChanged, spec §5.3, and one test comment) with no functional changes.

repo: FileUpload is a stateful component (runs the upload lifecycle), unlike checkbox — pure-controlled semantics are architecturally incompatible because every lifecycle transition (StartUpload, UploadProgress, Complete, Error) reads the visible queue via get(), which would be the parent's prop in pure-controlled mode, breaking auto_upload and progress visibility.

The ars-leptos adapter's use_machine only calls Service::set_props when props actually change (prev != new_props), so a veto (parent re-rendering with the same files prop) produces no event — hybrid resync is not achievable without external signaling.

The component-owns-queue contract matches react-dropzone and similar libraries: the component is the source of truth for the working queue, on_files_change reports changes but the parent cannot veto them.

code.finding: set_props is gated on should_sync = prev != new_props, meaning no-op re-renders (parent veto pattern) never propagate into the machine.
commit_files performs sync_controlled so api.files() reflects the working queue immediately; this is the correct component-owns-queue behavior, not an optimistic hack.
Test comments previously described the behavior as 'optimistic sync' which was misleading; reframed to component-owns-queue terminology.

workflow: When a review comment conflicts with a previously approved design decision, surfacing the tension as a design fork question (rather than deciding unilaterally) avoided a fourth churn cycle on the controlled-mode model.
Architectural constraints (adapter prop-diffing, stateful lifecycle operations) should be investigated before committing to an implementation path — two user Q&A rounds were needed to converge.
Clippy enforces backtick-quoting for non-standard identifiers in doc comments (e.g., react-dropzone must be written as `react-dropzone`).

friction: The controlled-component model churned three times (hgY, 0CV/0CY, 0Ca) before the correct framing was established, indicating the component-owns-queue contract was not clearly specified upfront.
The hybrid resync option (user's first choice) was only ruled out after deeper investigation into the adapter's prop-diffing behavior — this wasted one Q&A round.
Pure-controlled was also considered and ruled out after tracing the full upload lifecycle, requiring a second clarifying Q&A.
open_items: The component-owns-queue contract is now documented, but the spec and code have been through multiple controlled-mode design reversals — worth a holistic review of §5 (controlled mode) in file-upload.md to ensure consistency.

If pure-controlled semantics are ever needed in the future, it would require a foundational rework: every file-touching transition (select, drop, remove, clear, and every status change) must emit on_files_change and round-trip through SetFiles.
